g.data-narration: Welcome to Pipeline Performance - a fundamental concept in computer organization that enables modern processors to achieve high instruction throughput.
g.text: university 2022 Scheme - Computer Organization and Architecture

g.data-narration: Pipelining is an implementation technique where multiple instructions are overlapped in execution, similar to an assembly line in manufacturing.
Without pipelining, a processor completes one instruction before starting the next. Pipelining dramatically improves throughput by processing different instructions in each stage simultaneously.
The speedup factor measures how much faster a pipelined processor is compared to non-pipelined. The formula is S = (n × k) / (k + n - 1).
g.text: Non-pipelined time: Tn = n × k × τ

g.data-narration: For example, a 5-stage pipeline executing 50 instructions with 20ns cycle time achieves 4.63x speedup, approaching the theoretical maximum of 5x.
g.text: Problem: Non-pipelined processor: 100ns/instr. 5-stage pipeline: 20ns/stage. Find speedup for 50 instructions.

g.data-narration: Pipeline efficiency measures how effectively the pipeline is utilized. It's the ratio of actual speedup to theoretical maximum, approaching 100% as instructions increase.
Throughput is the number of instructions completed per unit time. The ideal throughput is one instruction per clock cycle, often measured in MIPS.
Pipeline hazards are situations that prevent the next instruction from executing in the designated cycle. There are three main types: structural, data, and control hazards.
Structural hazards occur when hardware resources are insufficient. For example, if only one memory unit exists, it can't fetch instruction and load data simultaneously.
Data hazards occur when instructions depend on each other's results. RAW is the most common - trying to read before a previous instruction writes.
Control hazards occur due to branches. The processor can't determine the next instruction until the branch is resolved, causing pipeline stalls.
Average CPI considers pipeline stalls. For a 5-stage pipeline with 2-cycle branch penalty and 20% branches: average CPI = 1 + (0.20 × 2) = 1.4.
Latency is time for one instruction; throughput is instructions per time. Pipelining increases throughput dramatically while possibly slightly increasing latency.
Remember these key formulas: Speedup S = (n×k)/(k+n-1), Efficiency E = S/k, Throughput = 1/τ, Average CPI = ideal CPI + stall cycles.
Key takeaways: A k-stage pipeline achieves max speedup of k. Pipeline fill overhead matters for small n. Hazards (structural, data, control) reduce efficiency. Throughput is the primary benefit.
